frontmatter.title: Notes on models of type theory

mainmatter.p: We begin with some preliminaries that we will need later.

Let \mathcal {K} be a bicategory, and let A \equiv { \left ( l_A \dashv r_A \colon A_1 \to A_0 \right )} and B \equiv { \left ( l_B \dashv r_B \colon B_1 \to B_0 \right )} be a pair of adjunctions in \mathcal {K}. Fix a pair of morphisms { A_0 } \xrightarrow {{ f_0 }}{ B_0 } and { A_1 } \xrightarrow {{ f_1 }}{ B_1 }.

ol.li: Given a 2-cell { l_B \circ f_0 } \xrightarrow {{ \lambda }}{ f_1 \circ l_A } as depicted on the left below, its right mate { f_0 \circ r_A } \xrightarrow {{ \rho }}{ r_B \circ f_1 } is obtained by bending with the unit of B and the counit of A as shown below on the right:

Given a 2-cell { f_0 \circ r_A } \xrightarrow {{ \rho }}{ r_B \circ f_1 } as depicted on the right below, its left mate { l_B \circ f_0 } \xrightarrow {{ \lambda }}{ f_1 \circ l_A } is obtained by bending with the unit of A and the counit of B as shown below on the left:

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal {K} be a bicategory, and let A \equiv { \left ( l_A \dashv r_A \colon A_1 \to A_0 \right )} and B \equiv { \left ( l_B \dashv r_B \colon B_1 \to B_0 \right )} be a pair of adjunctions in \mathcal {K}. Then a (left and right) lax morphism { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } between the two adjunctions is given by a pair of maps { A_0 } \xrightarrow {{ f_0 }}{ B_0 } and { A_1 } \xrightarrow {{ f_1 }}{ B_1 } together with a pair of mates { l_B \circ f_0 } \xrightarrow {{ l_f }}{ f_1 \circ l_A } and { f_0 \circ r_A } \xrightarrow {{ r_f }}{ r_B \circ f_1 } as depicted below:

ol.li: When the left mate is invertible, we say that { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } is (left strong) and right lax.
When the right mate is invertible, we say that { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } is (right strong) and left lax.
When both mates are invertible, we say that { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } is strong.

frontmatter.title: Strong morphisms of adjunctions and the Beck–Chevalley condition

mainmatter.p: The condition that a lax morphism of adjunctions be right strong is sometimes referred to as the Beck–Chevalley condition, e.g. by Uemura. Indeed, the right-hand mates in a lax morphism of adjunctions are occasionally referred to as Beck–Chevalley transformations.

Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a morphism in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}, and let \Gamma be a representable object. We shall use the following notations inspired by dependent type theory to refer to elements of U and E:

ol.li: We shall write { \left ( \Gamma \vdash _p A \right )} to refer to a morphism { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ A }}{ U }.

Given { \left ( \Gamma \vdash _p A \right )}, we shall write { \left ( \Gamma \vdash _p a : A \right )} to refer to a morphism { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ a }}{ E } such that the composite \Gamma \xrightarrow {a}E \xrightarrow {p}U is equal to { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ A }}{ U }.

mainmatter.p: A morphism { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } in \operatorname {Pr} { \mathcal {C} } is called representable when for every \Gamma \vdash _p A, the fiber product \Gamma .A : \equiv E \times _U \Gamma is representable:

Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable morphism in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}. Given { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ \Gamma } and \Delta \vdash a : A \gamma, we shall write { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ { \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle } }}{ \Gamma .A } for the universal map below:

Given { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ \Gamma }, \Delta \vdash B, \Gamma \vdash A, and \Delta .B \vdash a : A \gamma p_B, we shall write { \Delta .B } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma .a }}{ \Gamma .A } for the universal map { \left \langle \gamma \circ p_B,a \right \rangle }.

frontmatter.title: The adjunction determined by a representable map
The generic elements functor of a representable map

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}. We shall define the generic elements functor { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } that, intuitively, sends an element of the base to the “generic element” of the fiber of { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } at that element.

li.p: Fixing an element \Gamma \vdash A, we define qA to be the upstairs map in the following pullback square that exists by representability of { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }, and can be chosen by the univalence principle for the category \mathcal {C}:

Fixing a morphism { A } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma }}{ B } in \widetilde { U } over { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ \Gamma } in \mathcal {C}, we choose { qA } \xrightarrow {{ q \bar \gamma }}{ qB } to be the upper triangle determined by the universal property of the pullback as follows:

Commutation of the outer square follows from the homomorphism property of { A } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma }}{ B } in \widetilde { U }. The assignment preserves identities and compositions, so we have defined a functor { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } }.

frontmatter.title: The weakening counit of a representable map

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}, so that { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } is the corresponding generic elements functor. We may define a natural transformation { \widetilde { p } \circ q } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon }}{ 1 _{ \widetilde { U } } } that we shall call the weakening counit of the representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }.
For \Gamma \vdash A we define the component { \widetilde { p } qA } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon _A }}{ A } to be the following cell over { \Gamma .A } \xrightarrow {{ p_A }}{ A }:

The reason to name \epsilon _A as we have done is that it witnesses the fact that “weakening” a given type \Gamma \vdash A into the context of its generic element gives you precisely the type of the generic element.

frontmatter.title: The substitution unit of a representable map

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}, so that { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } is the corresponding generic elements functor. We may define a natural transformation { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } } that we shall call the substitution unit of the representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }.
We define { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } } assigning to each \Gamma \vdash a : A the upper-hand depicted below:

The reason to refer to { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } } as the substitution unit is that it witnesses the fact that every element of a given type arises from that type’s generic element by substitution.

frontmatter.title: Snake identity: duplicating the generic element

mainmatter.p: The following “snake identity” for the substitution unit and the weakening counit holds for any representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}:
As we shall see in the proof below, this identity states that duplicating the generic element gives you the generic element (up to weakening).

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We first instantiate the snake diagram at an arbitrary { \left ( \Gamma \vdash A \right )} \in \widetilde { U }.

As { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \pi _E }}{ \mathcal {C} } is a discrete fibration, it suffices to check that the above diagram lies over an identity cell in \mathcal {C}. We therefore consider its image in \mathcal {C} as an endomorphism on \Gamma .A to check that it is the identity, computing as follows using the componentwise definitions of the substitution unit and the weakening counit:

These two maps evidently compose to the identity: indeed, by the universal property of \Gamma .A as the fiber product E \times _U \Gamma, it suffices to check that qA \circ p_A.qpqA \circ { \left \langle \Gamma .A,qA \right \rangle } = qA and p_A \circ p_A.qpqA \circ { \left \langle \Gamma .A,qA \right \rangle } = p_A, as can be read off from the diagrams below.

mainmatter.p: The following “snake identity” for the substitution unit and the weakening counit holds for any representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}:
As we shall see, this law states that any element \Gamma \vdash a : A gives rise to a section of the display map { \Gamma .A } \xrightarrow {{ p_A }}{ \Gamma }.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We may instantiate the snake diagram at an arbitrary { \left ( \Gamma \vdash a:A \right )} \in \widetilde { E }, and we may whisker on the right with { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ \pi _U }}{ \mathcal {C} } which, as a discrete fibration, is a faithful functor. It suffices now to check that the resulting cell is an identity map \mathcal {C}. We we compute it below using the componentwise definitions of the substitution unit and weakening counit.

The cell depicted above is indeed an identity, as { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ \Gamma .a }}{ \Gamma .A } is defined to be the section of { \Gamma .A } \xrightarrow {{ p_A }}{ \Gamma } determined by { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ a }}{ E }.

frontmatter.title: Adjointness of the generic elements functor

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} { \mathcal {C} }; then we have an adjunction \widetilde { p } \dashv q where { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } is the generic elements functor of { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }, in which the unit and counit are the substitution unit and weakening counit respectively.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We have verified the two snake identities in Lemmas [jms-00DP] and [jms-00DQ] respectively.

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a morphism in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}; if the induced functor { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \widetilde { p } }}{ \widetilde { U } } between categories of elements has a right adjoint, then { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } is representable.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let there be a right adjoint { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } to { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \widetilde { p } }}{ \widetilde { U } }, and fix { \left ( \Gamma \vdash A \right )} \in \widetilde { U }. Applying the right adjoint, we have an element { \left ( \Gamma .A \vdash qA \right )} \in \widetilde { E }; instantiating the counit at { \left ( \Gamma \vdash A \right )}, we have a cell { \widetilde { p } qA } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon _{A} }}{ A }, which amounts to a commuting square in the following configuration:

It suffices to show that the square depicted above is cartesian. Because pullbacks in presheaf categories are computed pointwise, it suffices to consider only squares with a representable apex, such as the one below in which \Delta is assumed representable:

To construct the universal map { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ { \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle } }}{ \Gamma .A } factoring \gamma ,a through qA,p_A respectively, we note the square above corresponds precisely to a morphism { \widetilde { p } a } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma }}{ A } in \widetilde { U }, visualised as follows:
We can transpose { \widetilde { p } a } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ A } to obtain the following { a } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma ^ \sharp }}{ qA } in \widetilde { E }:

We now define { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ { \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle } }}{ \Gamma .A } to be the image of { a } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma ^ \sharp }}{ qA } in \mathcal {C}, i.e. { \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle } : \equiv \pi _E \bar \gamma ^ \sharp.The factorisation qA \circ { \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle } = a holds by inspection of the above diagram in \widetilde { E }. The other factorisation p_A \circ { \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle } = \gamma holds by the snake identities of adjoints:

Uniqueness of { a } \xrightarrow {{ { \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle } }}{ qA } with this property can be seen as follows. We fix any { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ h }}{ \Gamma .A } satisfying the two factorisation properties; in other words, we assume that h lies underneath a morphism in { a } \xrightarrow {{ \bar {h} }}{ qA } in \widetilde { E } satisfying (only) the additional property that \epsilon _A \circ \bar {h} = \gamma:
To see that h = { \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle }, it therefore suffices to observe the following instance of the snake identity:

mainmatter.p: A morphism { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } is representable in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C} if and only if the induced functor { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \widetilde { p } }}{ \widetilde { U } } has a right adjoint.

We are now ready to describe natural models. The purpose of the preceding lemmas was to provide a fully algebraic and diagrammatic description of natural models as an alternative to the usual presheaf-theoretic description. The difference between these descriptions begins to matter when organizing natural models into a (2,1)-category — what seems arbitrary in the language of presheaves becomes completely natural in the diagrammatic language, so much so that someone who has seen the diagrammatic definition of natural models would not find it difficult to reconstruct from scratch the appropriate notion of morphism.

frontmatter.title: Natural models of dependent type theory

mainmatter.p: A natural model is defined to be a small category \mathcal {C} with a terminal object together with a representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}.

frontmatter.title: Algebraic description of the bicategory of natural models

mainmatter.p: A natural model is described by the following structure in the bicategory of (small) categories:

ol.li: a sequence of discrete fibrations E \xrightarrow {p} U \xrightarrow {c} \mathcal {C}:
a right adjoint { \mathbf {1} } \xrightarrow {{ \diamond }}{ \mathcal {C} } to the terminal functor { \mathcal {C} } \xrightarrow {{ * }}{ \mathbf {1} }:
a right adjoint { U } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ E } to { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }:

mainmatter.p: Let M and N be natural models, as depicted schematically below:
A lax homomorphism { M } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi }}{ N } of natural models is given by the following data:

The last cell { * _{ N } \circ \Phi _{ \mathcal {C} } } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi _{ * } }}{ * _{ M } } always exists canonically and is invertible, but we include it as it plays a role in further definitions.

A lax homomorphism { M } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi }}{ N } is called strong when the 2-cells \Phi _{ p } and \Phi _{ c } are invertible, and the right mates \Phi _{ q } , \Phi _{ \diamond } of \Phi _{ p } , \Phi _{ * } depicted below are invertible, ensuring that context extension and the empty contexts are preserved respectively:

frontmatter.title: Transformations of homomorphisms of natural models

mainmatter.p: Let M and N be natural models, as depicted schematically below:

Let { M } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi , \Psi }}{ N } be two (lax) homomorphisms from M to N. A transformation { \Phi } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha }}{ \Psi } of homomorphisms from M to N is by three cells
such that commute with the components of \Phi and \Psi in the following sense:

It is seen by drawing string diagrams that if the underlying cells of a transformation of homomorphisms of natural models are invertible, then the transformation itself is invertible. Furthermore, it follows from the discreteness of E and U over \mathcal {C} that the transformation laws uniquely determine the components \alpha _E and \alpha _U if they exist. As such, the only data in a transformation is the lowest cell \alpha _{ \mathcal {C}} acting on contexts.

There are several possible bicategories of natural models, corresponding to different allowances of laxness. All of these are, in fact, (strict) 2-categories — but we see this as a meta-level statement as it concerns definitional equality.

By “the” bicategory of natural models, we shall refer to the one in which 1-cells are strong homomorphisms and the 2-cells are invertible transformations. This (2,1)-category is precisely the one described by Uemura; although it is certainly interesting to consider other variations, the (2,1)-category that we consider here has various important properties including presentability in the sense of Lurie. We hope that the simplified picture of natural models offered by string diagrams will facilitate the study of the entire landscape of bicategories of natural models.
A natural model is described by the following structure in the bicategory of (small) categories:

ol.li: a sequence of discrete fibrations E \xrightarrow {p} U \xrightarrow {c} \mathcal {C}:
a right adjoint { \mathbf {1} } \xrightarrow {{ \diamond }}{ \mathcal {C} } to the terminal functor { \mathcal {C} } \xrightarrow {{ * }}{ \mathbf {1} }:
a right adjoint { U } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ E } to { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }:

mainmatter.p: Let M and N be natural models, as depicted schematically below:
A lax homomorphism { M } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi }}{ N } of natural models is given by the following data:

The last cell { * _{ N } \circ \Phi _{ \mathcal {C} } } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi _{ * } }}{ * _{ M } } always exists canonically and is invertible, but we include it as it plays a role in further definitions.

A lax homomorphism { M } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi }}{ N } is called strong when the 2-cells \Phi _{ p } and \Phi _{ c } are invertible, and the right mates \Phi _{ q } , \Phi _{ \diamond } of \Phi _{ p } , \Phi _{ * } depicted below are invertible, ensuring that context extension and the empty contexts are preserved respectively:
A natural model is defined to be a small category \mathcal {C} with a terminal object together with a representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}.

Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a morphism in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}, and let \Gamma be a representable object. We shall use the following notations inspired by dependent type theory to refer to elements of U and E:

ol.li: We shall write { \left ( \Gamma \vdash _p A \right )} to refer to a morphism { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ A }}{ U }.

Given { \left ( \Gamma \vdash _p A \right )}, we shall write { \left ( \Gamma \vdash _p a : A \right )} to refer to a morphism { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ a }}{ E } such that the composite \Gamma \xrightarrow {a}E \xrightarrow {p}U is equal to { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ A }}{ U }.

mainmatter.p: A morphism { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } in \operatorname {Pr} { \mathcal {C} } is called representable when for every \Gamma \vdash _p A, the fiber product \Gamma .A : \equiv E \times _U \Gamma is representable:

Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable morphism in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}. Given { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ \Gamma } and \Delta \vdash a : A \gamma, we shall write { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ { \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle } }}{ \Gamma .A } for the universal map below:

Given { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ \Gamma }, \Delta \vdash B, \Gamma \vdash A, and \Delta .B \vdash a : A \gamma p_B, we shall write { \Delta .B } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma .a }}{ \Gamma .A } for the universal map { \left \langle \gamma \circ p_B,a \right \rangle }.

frontmatter.title: The adjunction determined by a representable map
The generic elements functor of a representable map

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}. We shall define the generic elements functor { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } that, intuitively, sends an element of the base to the “generic element” of the fiber of { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } at that element.

li.p: Fixing an element \Gamma \vdash A, we define qA to be the upstairs map in the following pullback square that exists by representability of { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }, and can be chosen by the univalence principle for the category \mathcal {C}:

Fixing a morphism { A } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma }}{ B } in \widetilde { U } over { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ \Gamma } in \mathcal {C}, we choose { qA } \xrightarrow {{ q \bar \gamma }}{ qB } to be the upper triangle determined by the universal property of the pullback as follows:

Commutation of the outer square follows from the homomorphism property of { A } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma }}{ B } in \widetilde { U }. The assignment preserves identities and compositions, so we have defined a functor { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } }.

frontmatter.title: The weakening counit of a representable map

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}, so that { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } is the corresponding generic elements functor. We may define a natural transformation { \widetilde { p } \circ q } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon }}{ 1 _{ \widetilde { U } } } that we shall call the weakening counit of the representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }.
For \Gamma \vdash A we define the component { \widetilde { p } qA } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon _A }}{ A } to be the following cell over { \Gamma .A } \xrightarrow {{ p_A }}{ A }:

The reason to name \epsilon _A as we have done is that it witnesses the fact that “weakening” a given type \Gamma \vdash A into the context of its generic element gives you precisely the type of the generic element.

frontmatter.title: The substitution unit of a representable map

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}, so that { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } is the corresponding generic elements functor. We may define a natural transformation { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } } that we shall call the substitution unit of the representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }.
We define { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } } assigning to each \Gamma \vdash a : A the upper-hand depicted below:

The reason to refer to { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } } as the substitution unit is that it witnesses the fact that every element of a given type arises from that type’s generic element by substitution.

frontmatter.title: Snake identity: duplicating the generic element

mainmatter.p: The following “snake identity” for the substitution unit and the weakening counit holds for any representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}:
As we shall see in the proof below, this identity states that duplicating the generic element gives you the generic element (up to weakening).

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We first instantiate the snake diagram at an arbitrary { \left ( \Gamma \vdash A \right )} \in \widetilde { U }.

As { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \pi _E }}{ \mathcal {C} } is a discrete fibration, it suffices to check that the above diagram lies over an identity cell in \mathcal {C}. We therefore consider its image in \mathcal {C} as an endomorphism on \Gamma .A to check that it is the identity, computing as follows using the componentwise definitions of the substitution unit and the weakening counit:

These two maps evidently compose to the identity: indeed, by the universal property of \Gamma .A as the fiber product E \times _U \Gamma, it suffices to check that qA \circ p_A.qpqA \circ { \left \langle \Gamma .A,qA \right \rangle } = qA and p_A \circ p_A.qpqA \circ { \left \langle \Gamma .A,qA \right \rangle } = p_A, as can be read off from the diagrams below.

mainmatter.p: The following “snake identity” for the substitution unit and the weakening counit holds for any representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}:
As we shall see, this law states that any element \Gamma \vdash a : A gives rise to a section of the display map { \Gamma .A } \xrightarrow {{ p_A }}{ \Gamma }.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We may instantiate the snake diagram at an arbitrary { \left ( \Gamma \vdash a:A \right )} \in \widetilde { E }, and we may whisker on the right with { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ \pi _U }}{ \mathcal {C} } which, as a discrete fibration, is a faithful functor. It suffices now to check that the resulting cell is an identity map \mathcal {C}. We we compute it below using the componentwise definitions of the substitution unit and weakening counit.

The cell depicted above is indeed an identity, as { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ \Gamma .a }}{ \Gamma .A } is defined to be the section of { \Gamma .A } \xrightarrow {{ p_A }}{ \Gamma } determined by { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ a }}{ E }.

frontmatter.title: Adjointness of the generic elements functor

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} { \mathcal {C} }; then we have an adjunction \widetilde { p } \dashv q where { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } is the generic elements functor of { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }, in which the unit and counit are the substitution unit and weakening counit respectively.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We have verified the two snake identities in Lemmas [jms-00DP] and [jms-00DQ] respectively.

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a morphism in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}; if the induced functor { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \widetilde { p } }}{ \widetilde { U } } between categories of elements has a right adjoint, then { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } is representable.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let there be a right adjoint { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } to { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \widetilde { p } }}{ \widetilde { U } }, and fix { \left ( \Gamma \vdash A \right )} \in \widetilde { U }. Applying the right adjoint, we have an element { \left ( \Gamma .A \vdash qA \right )} \in \widetilde { E }; instantiating the counit at { \left ( \Gamma \vdash A \right )}, we have a cell { \widetilde { p } qA } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon _{A} }}{ A }, which amounts to a commuting square in the following configuration:

It suffices to show that the square depicted above is cartesian. Because pullbacks in presheaf categories are computed pointwise, it suffices to consider only squares with a representable apex, such as the one below in which \Delta is assumed representable:

To construct the universal map { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ { \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle } }}{ \Gamma .A } factoring \gamma ,a through qA,p_A respectively, we note the square above corresponds precisely to a morphism { \widetilde { p } a } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma }}{ A } in \widetilde { U }, visualised as follows:
We can transpose { \widetilde { p } a } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ A } to obtain the following { a } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma ^ \sharp }}{ qA } in \widetilde { E }:

We now define { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ { \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle } }}{ \Gamma .A } to be the image of { a } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma ^ \sharp }}{ qA } in \mathcal {C}, i.e. { \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle } : \equiv \pi _E \bar \gamma ^ \sharp.The factorisation qA \circ { \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle } = a holds by inspection of the above diagram in \widetilde { E }. The other factorisation p_A \circ { \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle } = \gamma holds by the snake identities of adjoints:

Uniqueness of { a } \xrightarrow {{ { \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle } }}{ qA } with this property can be seen as follows. We fix any { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ h }}{ \Gamma .A } satisfying the two factorisation properties; in other words, we assume that h lies underneath a morphism in { a } \xrightarrow {{ \bar {h} }}{ qA } in \widetilde { E } satisfying (only) the additional property that \epsilon _A \circ \bar {h} = \gamma:
To see that h = { \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle }, it therefore suffices to observe the following instance of the snake identity:

mainmatter.p: A morphism { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } is representable in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C} if and only if the induced functor { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \widetilde { p } }}{ \widetilde { U } } has a right adjoint.

frontmatter.title: Abstract and Concrete Type Theories
frontmatter.meta: @phdthesis{uemura-2021-thesis, author = {Uemura, Taichi}, address = {Amsterdam}, publisher = {Institute for Logic, Language and Computation}, school = {Universiteit van Amsterdam}, url = {https://www.illc.uva.nl/cms/Research/Publications/Dissertations/DS-2021-09.text.pdf}, year = {2021}, title = {Abstract and Concrete Type Theories}, }

frontmatter.title: Natural models of homotopy type theory
frontmatter.meta: @article{awodey-2018-mscs, author = {Awodey, Steve}, publisher = {Cambridge University Press}, year = {2018}, doi = {10.1017/S0960129516000268}, eprint = {1406.3219}, eprintclass = {math.CT}, eprinttype = {arXiv}, journal = {Mathematical Structures in Computer Science}, number = {2}, pages = {241--286}, title = {Natural models of homotopy type theory}, volume = {28}, }

mainmatter.p: The notion of a natural model of type theory is defined in terms of that of a representable natural transformation of presheaves. It is shown that such models agree exactly with the concept of a category with families in the sense of Dybjer, which can be regarded as an algebraic formulation of type theory. We determine conditions for such models to satisfy the inference rules for dependent sums Σ, dependent products Π and intensional identity types Id, as used in homotopy type theory. It is then shown that a category admits such a model if it has a class of maps that behave like the abstract fibrations in axiomatic homotopy theory: They should be stable under pullback, closed under composition and relative products, and there should be weakly orthogonal factorizations into the class. It follows that many familiar settings for homotopy theory also admit natural models of the basic system of homotopy type theory.

frontmatter.title: Discrete generalised polynomial functors
frontmatter.meta: @misc{fiore-2012-icalp, author = {Fiore, Marcelo P.}, url = {https://www.cl.cam.ac.uk/~mpf23/talks/ICALP2012.pdf}, year = {2012}, note = {Slides from talk given at ICALP 2012}, title = {Discrete generalised polynomial functors}, }

frontmatter.title: Higher topos theory
frontmatter.meta: @book{lurie-htt, author = {Lurie, Jacob}, publisher = {Princeton University Press}, year = {2009}, eprint = {math/0608040}, eprintclass = {math.CT}, eprinttype = {arXiv}, isbn = {978-0-691-14049-0}, title = {Higher Topos Theory}, }

mainmatter.p: Higher category theory is generally regarded as technical and forbidding, but part of it is considerably more tractable: the theory of infinity-categories, higher categories in which all higher morphisms are assumed to be invertible. In Higher Topos Theory, Jacob Lurie presents the foundations of this theory, using the language of weak Kan complexes introduced by Boardman and Vogt, and shows how existing theorems in algebraic topology can be reformulated and generalized in the theory’s new language. The result is a powerful theory with applications in many areas of mathematics.

The book’s first five chapters give an exposition of the theory of infinity-categories that emphasizes their role as a generalization of ordinary categories. Many of the fundamental ideas from classical category theory are generalized to the infinity-categorical setting, such as limits and colimits, adjoint functors, ind-objects and pro-objects, locally accessible and presentable categories, Grothendieck fibrations, presheaves, and Yoneda’s lemma. A sixth chapter presents an infinity-categorical version of the theory of Grothendieck topoi, introducing the notion of an infinity-topos, an infinity-category that resembles the infinity-category of topological spaces in the sense that it satisfies certain axioms that codify some of the basic principles of algebraic topology. A seventh and final chapter presents applications that illustrate connections between the theory of higher topoi and ideas from classical topology.
